query: aerial oxygen saturation level as a marker for cyanotic spells in children with tetralogy of Fallot

retrieved_docs.title: Newborn Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Pulse Oximetry
retrieved_docs.content: Below is a list of the critical congenital heart defects that are the target of pulse oximetry screening. [4] Pulmonary atresia Tricuspid atresia Truncus arteriosus Total anomalous pulmonary venous return Hypoplastic left heart syndrome D-Transposition of the great vessels Double outlet right ventricle Ebstein anomaly Interrupted aortic arch Single ventricle complex Coarctation of the aorta Tetralogy of Fallot Below is a list of the diseases and disorders included on the Recommended Uniform Screening Panel (RUSP) as of July 2018. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- History and Physical -- Clinical Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: "Tet spells" or hypercyanotic episodes can occur in infants or toddlers with unrepaired TOF, characterized by tachypnea and hyperpnea and a decrease in the intensity of the cardiac murmur. Some patients will be inconsolable in addition to worsening cyanosis. These episodes can either subside spontaneously or can cause syncope with or without cardiac arrest. Thus, rapid intervention should be provided with a referral for prompt primary repair or palliation. If the patient survives such episodes but remains unrepaired, "Tet spells" tend to become rare and may disappear by 4 to 5 years of age. Presently, patients with TOF presenting with cyanosis and severe, longstanding cyanosis are rare in developed countries with adequate pediatric cardiac services. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Complications -- Mortality in Unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot
retrieved_docs.content: The most frequent causes of mortality in patients with no surgical intervention include hypoxic spells (68%), cerebrovascular accidents (17%), and brain abscesses (13%). Within the first year of life, 25% of infants with severe RVOTO die if left untreated, 40% by 3 years of age, 70% by 10 years of age, and 95% by 40 years of age. [57] [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanotic Heart Disease -- Evaluation -- Summary of CCHD Evaluation Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Right Heart Obstructive Lesions Pulmonary atresia (PA) Tricuspid atresia Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) Critical pulmonary stenosis

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanotic Heart Disease -- Evaluation -- Summary of CCHD Evaluation Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Positive CCHD screening with oxygen saturation less than 90% without a difference in oxygenation between upper and lower extremities. Hyperoxia test is positive in these conditions. Chest x-ray shows decreased or normal pulmonary blood flow. ECG is an important tool to differentiate among these lesions. Left axis (0 to 90 degrees) is characteristic of pulmonary atresia; left superior axis (0 to -90 degrees) for tricuspid atresia, and right axis (90 to 180 degrees) for critical pulmonary stenosis and TOF. Left Heart Obstructive Lesions Hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS) Interrupted aortic arch (IAA)/coarctation of the aorta (CoA) Critical aortic stenosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Atresia With Ventricular Septal Defect -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Pulse oximetry evaluates oxygen saturation, especially in dark-skinned infants, where cyanosis may be missed during the physical exam. A hyperoxia test conducted at birth can easily identify infants with cyanotic heart diseases and facilitate early management.

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The VSD in TOF is typically large or nonrestrictive, allowing pressure equalization within the ventricles. Therefore, whether the shunting is left to right or vice versa will depend on the degree of downstream pressure the blood flow encounters. With significant RVOTO, most of the blood will shunt toward the aorta, thereby bypassing the pulmonary bed with consequent cyanosis. Conversely, if the resistance across the RVOT is less than the systemic vascular resistance, most of the blood will be directed to the pulmonary vascular bed, and the patient will be minimally cyanotic or acyanotic (see Image. Tetralogy of Fallot). [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Management of Patients With Congenital Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Tetralogy of Fallot
retrieved_docs.content: These children may develop hypercyanotic spells, also known as tet spells, due to a sudden increase in the obstruction of the right outflow tract either due to agitation or a sharp drop in peripheral vascular resistance, which may occur after feeding. [49] Management of a hypoxic-hypercyanotic spell includes: Sedation with ketamine or opiates, benzodiazepines Oxygen application Increase peripheral systemic resistance by Jack-knife position- pressing the child's flexed knee against the chest Infusion of vasoconstrictors to increase peripheral vascular resistance generous volume bolus (e.g., 20 to 50 ml/kg) Compensation for metabolic acidosis by buffering Beta-blockers [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanotic Heart Disease -- Evaluation -- Summary of CCHD Evaluation Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Positive CCHD screening with oxygen saturation less than 95%; there is a greater than 3% difference between upper and lower oxygen saturation. Negative hyperoxia test. Positive BP gradient between upper and lower extremities is seen in this lesions, except for HLHS. Chest x-ray shows increase pulmonary blood flow. ECG shows normal axis for a newborn (90 to 180 degrees), except for critical aortic stenosis which has left axis for a newborn (0 to 90 degrees). Mixing Lesions Transposition of the great arteries (TGA) Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR) Truncus arteriosus Cyanosis presents from mixing pulmonary and systemic blood flow. These conditions are considered Ductal-independent lesions. PDA is not required, but they can present with or without PFO. Screening Findings

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanotic Heart Disease -- Evaluation -- Summary of CCHD Evaluation Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Positive CCHD screening with oxygen saturation less than 95%. There is no difference between upper and lower oxygen saturation, except for d-TGA with pulmonary hypertension or coarctation of the aorta, where O2 saturation is higher in the foot than in the right arm. Negative Hyperoxia Test. Chest x-ray shows normal to increased pulmonary blood flow. ECG shows normal axis for a newborn (90 to 180 degrees).

retrieved_docs.title: Cyanotic Heart Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The seven primary CCHD screening targets are hypoplastic left heart syndrome (HLHS), pulmonary atresia (PA), Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF), total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR), transposition of great arteries (TGA), tricuspid atresia, and truncus arteriosus. Secondary screening targets are coarctation of the aorta (CoA), interrupted aortic arch (IAA), critical aortic stenosis, DORV, Ebstein anomaly and single ventricle complex. CCHD screening would miss about 15% of all CCHD cases; COA/IAA, TAPVR and TOF cases are the most common conditions missed. A failed newborn screen may also indicate other disease processes, such as pulmonary hypertension, primary pulmonary parenchymal disease, or hemoglobinopathies. Hyperoxia Test

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Neonates with severe RVOTO who present with profound hypoxemia and cyanosis will require prostaglandin therapy to maintain ductal patency and adequate pulmonary blood flow before a transcatheter intervention or surgical repair. Tet spells require a rapid and aggressive approach, including knee-chest positioning to increase systemic vascular resistance and "force" more blood into the pulmonary artery, oxygen therapy to cause pulmonary vasodilation, and decrease pulmonary vascular resistance. Patients may require intravenous fluid boluses to improve RV filling and increase pulmonary blood flow. They may also need intravenous beta-blockers to decrease heart rate to aid RV filling further and, sometimes, intravenous phenylephrine to increase systemic afterload. Morphine can also be given as a sedative during a tet spell, alleviating pain and anxiety and helping decrease heart and respiratory rates. A tet spell, left untreated, may lead to loss of consciousness and, ultimately, death. [25] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Different factors can contribute to RVOTO, including a stenotic pulmonary valve, a hypoplastic pulmonary valve annulus, the deviation of the infundibular septum that causes subvalvular obstruction, and the hypertrophy of the muscular bands of the RVOT. The obstruction across the RVOT can also be dynamic with sudden increases in cyanosis, a phenomenon known as “Tet or hypoxic spells.” The physiological process surrounding these hypercyanotic episodes is yet to be fully understood but appears to involve a decrease in systemic vascular resistance or an increase in pulmonary vascular resistance. The result is an increase in the right-to-left shunting across the ventricular septal defect, which produces marked desaturation. These episodes can be triggered by agitation, pain, anemia, fever, hypovolemia, or peripheral vasodilatation. These hypoxic spells occur in infants or children but do not occur in adults. [14] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Tetralogy of Fallot -- History and Physical -- Clinical Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical presentation varies based on the severity of the RVOTO, most commonly presenting as a cyanotic neonate. Adult patients with TOF may have dyspnea and exercise intolerance. With the advent of fetal echocardiography, the diagnosis can be made prenatally, and in infants who present with severe RVOTO, prompt stabilization can avoid profound cyanosis and rapid deterioration. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Innocent Murmur -- History and Physical -- Vital Signs
retrieved_docs.content: Oxygen saturation is also important. Cyanotic heart lesions may not present at birth, especially ductal-dependent lesions or Tetralogy of Fallot. If intra/extra-cardiac shunting is suspected then upper and lower extremity pulse oximetry should be measured. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracardiac Shunts -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A chest X-ray could show characteristic heart shapes in different defects. TOF could make the heart boot-shaped on CXR, TAPVR could show a snowman-shaped heart, and TGA could give the heart an "egg-on-string" appearance. [11] Blood work could show reactive polycythemia in cyanotic defects, indicating the severity of hypoxemia. Cardiac catheterization is not typically needed for diagnostic purposes but should be done once the surgical intervention is planned. It gives accurate pressures in heart chambers. Induction of oxygen or nitric oxide could be done in the pulmonary system to see vascular reactivity, which could help decide if the patient is a candidate for surgical intervention or pulmonary vasodilator therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1865
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is classified as a type of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder). The broader category اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ is itself a subcategory of الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia). In clinical terminology, الاِختِنَاق is mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating that asphyxia reflects a state of reduced oxygen concentration in the blood (hypoxemia). Understanding this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians recognize that adult respiratory failure and arrest represent specific manifestations of respiratory disorders that ultimately lead to or are associated with systemic hypoxemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with قساح. A principal manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (low blood oxygen), which is also mapped to الاِختِنَاق, indicating a link between hypoxemia and asphyxic events. Recognizing that patients with sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) may present with قساح and experience نقص الأكسجين في الدم that can progress to or be interpreted as الاِختِنَاق is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and timely therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is a specific form of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) and is directly linked to low oxygen levels in the blood (نقص الأكسجين في الدم), indicating that this breathing interruption can result in hypoxemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: valve pathologies, broader cardiac disease categories, diagnostic modalities and therapeutic interventions is essential for accurate diagnosis, optimal treatment planning and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hematologic disorder that can clinically manifest as adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest. This respiratory complication is classified under the broader category of respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which themselves are subtypes of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). The disease’s manifestation of low blood‑oxygen levels (نقص الأكسجين في الدم) reflects the same pathophysiologic process that defines asphyxia. Consequently, sickle cell anemia is linked to hypoxemia, which underlies the development of respiratory failure, situating the condition within a hierarchical framework that progresses from the specific hematologic disease to respiratory disorder and ultimately to the general concept of asphyxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1843
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation are classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني, which denotes fetal growth restriction. This condition is understood to be part of a broader, unspecified disorder that originates in the perinatal period, referred to as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Recognizing that تقييد النمو الجنيني falls within this larger perinatal context helps clinicians assess and manage infants presenting with these growth‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2612
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is classified as a hemoglobinopathy. In the context of this disease, respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is identified as a clinical complication, and the Arabic term الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia) is likewise listed as an allowed qualifier complication. Both respiratory failure and asphyxia are closely linked to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), which is a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The mapping of الاِختِنَاق to نقص الأكسجين في الدم highlights that low blood‑oxygen levels are a central pathophysiological feature that underlies these serious complications. Recognizing that sickle cell disease can precipitate severe respiratory compromise and hypoxemic states is essential for effective patient monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. This relationship underscores the importance of viewing individual symptoms as part of a larger, sometimes vague, clinical picture when assessing patients with ambiguous or poorly characterized disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.
